_meta.description: CS2103T iP increment requirements. Each entry maps a git tag to its increment details. This file is loaded by the AI reviewer at runtime.

fields.title: Human-readable name of the increment
fields.week: Course week in which this increment is introduced
fields.url: Link to official requirements page (optional, for reference link in review comment)
fields.description: What the increment is about (1-2 sentences)
fields.requirements: List of concrete things the student must implement
fields.constraints: List of explicit do-nots or restrictions mentioned in the requirements
fields.hints: Optional pedagogical hints the reviewer may reference

Level-0.title: Rename, Greet, Exit
Level-0.description: Rename the project from Duke to your own chatbot name, and implement a basic greeting and exit flow.
requirements: Rename the project/chatbot to a name of your choice (not Duke)
The chatbot greets the user when launched with a greeting message
The chatbot exits cleanly when the user types 'bye'
A farewell message is shown before the program exits
constraints: Do not keep the name 'Duke'
The program must not exit without showing a farewell message
hints: This increment is mostly about getting familiar with the codebase structure

Level-1.title: Echo
Level-1.description: Make the chatbot echo back whatever the user types until the user says bye.
requirements: The chatbot reads user input in a loop
Each user input is echoed back exactly as typed
The loop exits when the user types 'bye'
constraints: Input must be read from standard input (Scanner or similar)

Level-2.title: Add, List
Level-2.description: Allow the user to add tasks to a list and display all tasks.
requirements: The user can add a task by typing any text (treated as a task description)
The chatbot confirms each addition with a message showing the task was added
The user can type 'list' to see all tasks added so far
Tasks are displayed numbered (1., 2., 3. ...)
constraints: At this level all tasks are plain text — no types yet
Tasks should be stored in an in-memory list (no file persistence required yet)
hints: Consider using an ArrayList<String> or a fixed-size array

Level-3.title: Mark as Done
Level-3.description: Allow the user to mark tasks as done.
requirements: Each task has a done/not-done status
The user can type 'mark N' to mark task N as done
The user can type 'unmark N' to mark task N as not done
The 'list' command shows each task's status with an icon: [X] for done, [ ] for not done
constraints: Marking an already-done task again should not cause an error
hints: Introduce a Task class to hold description and status together

Level-4.title: ToDo, Event, Deadline
Level-4.description: Support three types of tasks: ToDo, Deadline, and Event, each with different attributes.
requirements: 'todo <description>' adds a ToDo task with no time constraint
'deadline <description> /by <date>' adds a Deadline task
'event <description> /from <start> /to <end>' adds an Event task
The 'list' command shows the task type: [T], [D], or [E] prefix
Each task type displays its additional attributes (by date, from/to times)
constraints: Typing plain text that is not a recognised command should produce an error message
Missing /by or /from /to should produce an informative error, not a crash
hints: This is a good place to use inheritance: Task as parent, ToDo/Deadline/Event as subclasses

A-TextUiTesting.title: Automated Text UI Testing
A-TextUiTesting.description: Set up automated text-based UI testing using input/expected-output file comparison.
requirements: A test input file (e.g. text-ui-test/input.txt) drives the chatbot
The chatbot's actual output is captured to a file
A script compares actual output to an expected output file and reports pass/fail
The test is runnable via a single command or script
constraints: Tests must be reproducible — same input must always produce same output
Do not hardcode file paths; use relative paths so tests work on any machine
hints: Check the project template for the runtest.bat / runtest.sh scripts provided

Level-5.title: Handle Errors
Level-5.description: Make the chatbot handle erroneous input gracefully without crashing.
requirements: Unknown commands produce a clear error message
Missing arguments (e.g. 'deadline' with no description) produce a specific error
Out-of-range task numbers produce an error message
The chatbot continues running after an error — it does not exit or throw an unhandled exception
constraints: Do not use bare catch(Exception e) as the only error handler
Error messages should be user-friendly, not stack traces
hints: Consider a custom DukeException class to distinguish expected errors from programming bugs

Level-6.title: Delete
Level-6.description: Allow the user to delete tasks from the list.
requirements: 'delete N' removes task N from the list
A confirmation message is shown after deletion, including the deleted task and the new list size
Task numbers update correctly after a deletion (no gaps)
constraints: Deleting a task number that does not exist should produce an error, not a crash

A-Enums.title: Enums
A-Enums.description: Refactor command parsing to use Java enums.
requirements: Define a Java enum for the recognised commands (e.g. BYE, LIST, MARK, UNMARK, TODO, DEADLINE, EVENT, DELETE)
Command parsing uses the enum instead of raw string comparisons
constraints: The enum must actually be used in the control flow, not just defined
hints: Enums can have methods and fields — you can attach a description or parser to each constant

Level-7.title: Save
Level-7.description: Persist the task list to disk so it survives between sessions.
requirements: Tasks are saved to a file on disk after every change (or at least on exit)
Tasks are loaded from the file on startup
If the file does not exist, the chatbot starts with an empty list (no error)
The file is stored in a data/ directory relative to the project root
constraints: Do not use absolute paths for the save file
The data/ directory should be created automatically if it does not exist
The file format must be parseable — plain text or a structured format you define
hints: Separate file I/O into a Storage class for clean separation of concerns

Level-8.title: Dates and Times
Level-8.description: Parse and store deadline and event dates as proper date/time objects.
requirements: Deadline /by and Event /from /to values are parsed into Java LocalDate or LocalDateTime objects
Dates are displayed in a human-friendly format (e.g. 'Oct 15 2024') rather than the raw input string
Invalid date formats produce a clear error message
constraints: Do not store dates as plain strings after this increment
At minimum support one input date format (e.g. yyyy-MM-dd)
hints: java.time.LocalDate and DateTimeFormatter are the idiomatic Java 8+ tools for this

A-MoreOOP.title: More OOP
A-MoreOOP.description: Refactor the codebase to improve OOP design by extracting responsibilities into separate classes.
requirements: Extract a Ui class responsible for all user-facing input/output
Extract a TaskList class to manage the collection of tasks
Extract a Parser class to interpret user commands
The main class (or a Duke class) orchestrates the above, but does not perform I/O or parsing directly
constraints: Each class should have a single, clear responsibility
Avoid static methods for core logic — use instances
hints: The goal is separation of concerns, not just moving code into new files

A-Packages.title: Packages
requirements: Classes are placed in a meaningful package (e.g. duke or your chatbot's name, all lowercase)
Package declarations appear at the top of each .java file
The project still compiles and runs correctly after the reorganisation
constraints: Package names must be lowercase per Java conventions

A-Gradle.title: Gradle
A-Gradle.description: Set up Gradle as the build tool for the project.
requirements: The project uses the Gradle build tool (build.gradle present)
'./gradlew run' launches the chatbot
'./gradlew build' compiles the project without errors
Dependencies (if any) are declared in build.gradle
constraints: Do not commit the .gradle/ cache directory — add it to .gitignore
The gradlew wrapper scripts must be committed so the build is reproducible
hints: The course provides a Gradle template — adapt it rather than writing from scratch

A-JUnit.title: JUnit Tests
A-JUnit.description: Write JUnit tests for at least two non-trivial methods.
requirements: At least two JUnit test classes exist under src/test/
Tests cover non-trivial logic (not just getters/setters)
'./gradlew test' runs all tests and they pass
constraints: Tests must not depend on system state or file system by default
Test class names should end in 'Test' per JUnit convention
hints: Good candidates: Parser (command parsing logic) and TaskList (add/delete/find logic)

A-Jar.title: JAR File
A-Jar.description: Package the application as a runnable JAR file.
requirements: './gradlew shadowJar' (or equivalent) produces a fat JAR in build/libs/
The JAR is runnable with 'java -jar <name>.jar' without any classpath flags
The chatbot works correctly when launched from the JAR
constraints: Do not commit the built JAR to the repository
The main class must be declared in the JAR manifest

A-JavaDoc.title: JavaDoc
A-JavaDoc.description: Add JavaDoc comments to public classes and methods.
requirements: All public classes have a class-level JavaDoc comment
All public methods have a JavaDoc comment with @param and @return tags where applicable
'./gradlew javadoc' generates documentation without errors or warnings
constraints: JavaDoc must describe intent and behaviour, not just restate the method name
Do not add JavaDoc to private/package-private members (not required)

A-CodingStandard.title: Coding Standard
A-CodingStandard.description: Ensure the codebase follows the CS2103T coding standard.
requirements: Class names are UpperCamelCase
Method and variable names are lowerCamelCase
Constants are UPPER_SNAKE_CASE
Braces follow the Java style (opening brace on same line)
No wildcard imports (import java.util.* not allowed)
Lines are not excessively long (max ~120 characters)
constraints: Refer to the CS2103T coding standard page for the full list of rules
hints: IntelliJ's 'Reformat Code' action handles most style issues automatically

Level-9.title: Find
Level-9.description: Allow the user to search for tasks by keyword.
requirements: 'find <keyword>' searches all tasks and lists those whose description contains the keyword
The results are displayed in a numbered list
If no tasks match, a message indicates no results were found
The search is case-insensitive (recommended, not strictly required)
constraints: The original task numbers in the full list must be preserved in find results so the user can act on them

A-AiAssisted.title: AI-Assisted Development
A-AiAssisted.description: Use an AI coding assistant to implement one increment and document the experience.
requirements: At least one increment is implemented with the help of an AI tool (e.g. GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, Claude)
A commit message or comment clearly indicates which parts were AI-assisted
A brief reflection is added (in a commit message, PR description, or README section) describing: what the AI produced, what you changed, and what you learned
constraints: You must understand and be able to explain every line of AI-generated code you commit
Do not commit AI output blindly without review
hints: The reflection is the learning artifact here — a thoughtful one-paragraph reflection is better than a long uncritical one
